title: American BBQ-Style Glazed Chicken (Tawa Method)

ingredients.name: chicken pieces (legs and thighs with bone)

subs.name: paneer cubes or cauliflower for a vegetarian BBQ option; reduce cook time to 15 minutes
jaggery (gur) dissolved in a little warm water, or brown sugar
1 tsp of a dark colour substitute such as Maggi Masala-e-Magic, or simply skip and add a bit more ketchup

steps.text: Score the chicken pieces with a sharp knife, cutting 2–3 deep gashes down to the bone. This allows the marinade to penetrate and speeds up cooking.

Combine the ketchup, honey, soy sauce, vinegar, garlic, ginger, chilli powder, black pepper and salt in a bowl. Stir until smooth. Reserve two tablespoons of this glaze for basting; use the rest to coat the chicken thoroughly. Marinate for at least 15 minutes.
For the tawa method: heat oil in a heavy tawa on medium flame. Place the chicken pieces in a single layer, cover with a lid (or upturned plate) and cook for 10 minutes until the bottom side colours.
Flip the chicken, brush generously with the reserved glaze and cover again. Cook for another 10 minutes on medium-low until the second side is coloured and the juices run clear.

Uncover, brush with any remaining glaze and raise the heat to medium-high for 3–4 minutes, turning once, until the sauce caramelises and the surface looks sticky and lacquered. Watch closely — honey burns quickly.
Rest the chicken for 5 minutes before serving. The juices will redistribute and the glaze will set into a glossy coat. Serve with coleslaw or rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: honey → jaggery (gur) dissolved in a little warm water, or brown sugar; soy-sauce → 1 tsp of a dark colour substitute such as Maggi Masala-e-Magic, or simply skip and add a bit more ketchup; chicken → paneer cubes or cauliflower for a vegetarian BBQ option; reduce cook time to 15 minutes
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A thick iron tawa or the bottom of a pressure cooker (without the lid locked) works perfectly as a substitute for a grill. The covered cooking step is key — it steams the chicken through before the final caramelise. For an oven option: 200°C for 30 minutes, brushing glaze on in the final 10 minutes. Soy sauce (Ching's brand) is widely available at Indian supermarkets.
